entities.description: Christine Thérèse Carpenter's two-volume unpublished doctoral dissertation, including a partial edition of the English Filius matris cycle transcribed from Harley MS 2276. U-M identifies volume 1, pages 230-359, as the locus associated with its digital text.
entities.label: Carpenter 1984 dissertation edition of the Lent and Palm Sunday sermons

rights.basis: The dissertation is a modern 1984 scholarly work and no open license or rights statement for a complete copy was found.
rights.component: Carpenter's dissertation edition, introduction, apparatus, commentary, transcribed text presentation, and other original scholarly contributions

reviewNotes: The primary transmission path is repository artifact to U-M encoding to OTA text to Carpenter dissertation edition to Harley MS 2276. U-M also consulted the dissertation directly when adding markup, so the graph includes a supporting U-M-to-dissertation relation.
CME90033 and OTA0033 are identifiers at different publication and upstream encoding layers; both are preserved as aliases without treating the artifacts as identical.

The publication override selects CME/source/recent_changes/additions_2023/CME90033.xml. The alternate CME/source/CME_from_OTA/CME90033.xml has a sparse header and different fixity, although canonicalized EEBO bodies compare byte-identically.

The broad work locus ff. 52v-70v follows U-M-added milestones. Every sermon after the first begins before its first internal folio milestone, so exact per-sermon opening loci remain partly inferred until manuscript images or Carpenter's edition can be inspected.

No public complete manuscript facsimile was found in the exact current BL record or linked legacy viewer as of 2026-07-14. This dated negative finding does not prove that no private, onsite, or unindexed reproduction exists.

Access and rights apply only to the entity and component named. They must not be inherited automatically by medieval text, manuscript images, Carpenter's edition, OTA text, U-M markup, or repository output.

lineage.summary: The approved General Corpus XML artifact is the full-header U-M CME90033 representation of a 2023 structured encoding. U-M obtained its text from Oxford Text Archive item 0033, preserved the OTA/Carpenter lineation, removed some OTA semantic conventions, and added structural divisions, line numbers, and folio references after comparison with Christine Thérèse Carpenter's 1984 dissertation. The OTA text is based on or closely associated with Carpenter's edition of seven Lent and Palm Sunday sermons transcribed from British Library Harley MS 2276, broadly ff. 52v-70v. The selected sermons belong to a larger fifteenth-century Middle English translation of the late-twelfth-century Latin Filius matris cycle.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalogSummary.date: Fifteenth-century translation; Harley witness circa 1450; edited in a 1984 dissertation
catalogSummary.title: Lent and Palm Sunday Sermons from the Middle English Filius Matris Cycle

catalogingSubject.label: Lent and Palm Sunday Sermons from the Middle English Filius Matris Cycle
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is Christine Thérèse Carpenter's selection of seven Middle English sermons associated with the five Sundays in Lent and Palm Sunday, as represented by OTA item 0033 and U-M CME90033. It is distinct from the complete Middle English Filius matris cycle, its late-twelfth-century Latin source cycle, British Library Harley MS 2276, Carpenter's two-volume dissertation, the OTA plain-text file, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: [Þis is] a Sermoun on þe First Sunday of Lentoun
contentParts.summary: An additional First-Sunday-in-Lent sermon on faith, hope, charity, confession, penance, vigils, fasting, prayer, and almsgiving.

contentParts.label: Þe first Sunday of Lentoun

locators.value: begins on f. 55r; internal U-M folio milestones run f. 55v-f. 57v

notes: The opening folio boundary is inferred from the preceding sermon's final milestone; it is not independently marked at this DIV1 start.
contentParts.summary: A First-Sunday-in-Lent gospel sermon centered on Christ's example, humility, fasting, and victory over temptation.

contentParts.label: Þe seconde Sunday of Lentoun

locators.value: begins on f. 57v; internal U-M folio milestones run f. 58r-f. 60r

notes: The opening folio boundary is inferred from the preceding sermon's final milestone.
contentParts.summary: A sermon on the Canaanite woman, faith, penance, humility, and perseverance in virtue.

contentParts.label: Þe þrid Sunday of Lentoun

locators.value: begins on f. 60r; internal U-M folio milestones run f. 60v-f. 63r

notes: The opening folio boundary is inferred from the preceding sermon's final milestone.
contentParts.summary: A gospel exposition on Christ's exorcism, spiritual interpretation, divine teaching, and resistance to vice.

contentParts.label: Þe fourþe Sunday of Lentoun

locators.value: begins on f. 63r; internal U-M folio milestones run f. 63v-f. 66r

notes: The opening folio boundary is inferred from the preceding sermon's final milestone.
contentParts.summary: An exposition of the feeding of the multitude and its spiritual meaning, including Christ as the bread of life.

contentParts.label: Þe fifþe Sunday of Lentoun

locators.value: begins on f. 66r; internal U-M folio milestones run f. 66v-f. 68v

notes: The opening folio boundary is inferred from the preceding sermon's final milestone.
contentParts.summary: A gospel sermon on Christ's controversy with his opponents, emphasizing patience, truth, and spiritual understanding.

contentParts.label: [Here bigynneþ be Gospel on] Palm Sunday

locators.value: begins on f. 68v; internal U-M folio milestones run f. 69r-f. 70v

notes: The opening folio boundary is inferred from the preceding sermon's final milestone.
contentParts.summary: An exposition of Christ's entry into Jerusalem, with instruction on obedience, humility, and virtuous conduct.

assertion.notes: The evidence supports only a broad century for the translation; the witness date and U-M's c.1425 stencil must not be transferred silently to composition.

extent.description: Seven DIV1 sermon units containing 116 prose paragraphs. The complete XML has 120 P elements when three header paragraphs and the one-paragraph argument are included. Computational inspection also found 2,229 numbered line milestones, 37 folio milestones, 2,077 LB elements, and 173 Q elements; the preserved lineation is editorial/reference layout, not verse.

formStatements.description: All seven sermons are continuous prose. The 2,229 numbered line milestones preserve an arbitrary edition-based reference system and do not indicate verse.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin is the language of the source cycle and of material Carpenter's apparatus reportedly supplied, but U-M says Latin glosses and side notes were omitted from this digital selection; Latin is therefore not asserted as an intrinsic language facet here.

The OTA catalog abstract says the file contains one sermon for each of five Lent Sundays plus Palm Sunday, but the downloadable file and U-M XML contain seven sermon blocks because the First Sunday in Lent has two separate sermons; the inspected text structure governs the item count.

The repository publication override binds CME90033.pdf to the full-header recent_changes/additions_2023 source. The alternate CME_from_OTA XML has a materially different header, although the canonicalized EEBO bodies are byte-identical; publication and upstream identifiers are therefore retained as aliases without conflating artifact layers.

Catalog and rights assertions belong to their named layers and do not transfer automatically among the medieval work, witness, dissertation, OTA text, U-M encoding, repository artifact, or any future manuscript photographs.

next_steps: Consult Carpenter's complete dissertation, Helen Spencer's 1977 study, and current scholarship on the complete English Filius matris tradition.
Keep witness production data separate from composition data.
openQuestions.question: Can the Middle English translation's date, locality, translator, or relationship among the surviving English witnesses be established more precisely?

next_steps: Inspect a Harley MS 2276 facsimile and Carpenter's dissertation.
Use the U-M-added milestones only for the broad ff. 52v-70v span until every DIV1 opening can be verified independently.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact manuscript start folios and dissertation page ranges for each of the seven sermons?

next_steps: Request or locate OTA deposit history and inspect Carpenter's dissertation preliminaries.
Preserve the three dated assertions at their own layers in the meantime.
openQuestions.question: How do the OTA record's 1980 issue date, the U-M header's 1983 OTA date, and the 1984 dissertation relate bibliographically?

notes: This broad cultural-origin statement does not claim a particular English region or reuse the witness's production place as an exact translation locality.

scope.description: The fifteenth-century English rendering of the selected Latin Filius matris sermons
The seven-sermon selection and its dissertation edition
The selected sermons in British Library Harley MS 2276, represented in dissertation volume 1, pages 230-359
The First through Fifth Sundays in Lent and Palm Sunday

summaries.value: A seven-sermon Middle English prose selection from the fifteenth-century translation of the Latin Filius matris cycle. It includes an additional penitential sermon and a gospel sermon for the First Sunday in Lent, then sermons for the Second through Fifth Sundays in Lent and Palm Sunday. The sermons combine exposition of liturgical Gospel readings with instruction on faith, humility, penance, fasting, prayer, almsgiving, obedience, patience, and other Christian virtues.

The selected text corresponds broadly to Harley MS 2276, folios 52v-70v, and to Carpenter's dissertation, volume 1, pages 230-359. The current BL catalog describes the larger witness as a sixty-sermon Middle English collection on folios 1r-146v, followed by an index on folios 146v-148v.

assertion.notes: A concise discovery title supplied for this edition-defined selection; it is not a medieval title.

titles.value: Lent and Palm Sunday Sermons from the Middle English Filius Matris Cycle

notes: The U-M 245-style title continues with a responsibility statement naming Carpenter and Harley MS 2276.
titles.value: Middle English version of the Matris Filius sermon cycle. Lent and Palm Sunday sermons

notes: Recorded as U-M's authority-style title; the Latin wording labels the Middle English translation rather than indicating that Latin is an intrinsic language of this selected text.
This is the dissertation title and is retained as an alternate title for the selection; the dissertation itself remains a separate lineage entity.
titles.value: An Edition of the Lent and Palm Sunday Sermons Contained in the Fifteenth-Century Translation of the Late Twelfth-Century Latin 'Filius Matris' Cycle

work.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
work.dateDisplay: Fifteenth-century translation; Harley witness circa 1450; edited in a 1984 dissertation
work.displayTitle: Lent and Palm Sunday Sermons from the Middle English Filius Matris Cycle
work.preferredTitle: Lent and Palm Sunday Sermons from the Middle English Filius Matris Cycle

publication.embeddedTitle: Middle English version of the Matris Filius sermon cycle. Lent and Palm Sunday sermons / transcribed from BL MS Harley 2276 by Christine T. Carpenter. — THE TEXT OF THE MIDDLE ENGLISH LENT AND PALM SUNDAY SERMONS — MED stencil c1425 *Matris Filius Serm.Cycle (Hrl 2276).

work.summary: A seven-sermon Middle English prose selection from the fifteenth-century translation of the Latin Filius matris cycle. It includes an additional penitential sermon and a gospel sermon for the First Sunday in Lent, then sermons for the Second through Fifth Sundays in Lent and Palm Sunday. The sermons combine exposition of liturgical Gospel readings with instruction on faith, humility, penance, fasting, prayer, almsgiving, obedience, patience, and other Christian virtues.
